topic: Properties of Matter — Pressure, Buoyancy, Bernoulli, Surface Tension & Viscosity

questions.text: Archimedes' Principle states that the buoyant force on a submerged body is equal to:
options: The weight of the body
The weight of the fluid displaced
The product of density and volume of the body
The difference in weight of body in air and in fluid
questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: Buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced by the submerged (or partially submerged) body. F_B = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g. This is the weight of the fluid that occupies the same volume as the submerged part of the body.

questions.text: Pascal's Law is the basis of which of the following?
options: Aeroplane wing lift
Hydraulic brakes and jacks
Capillary action in plants
Surface tension of water
questions.explanation: Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. This principle operates in hydraulic brakes (foot pressure transmitted equally to all wheels), hydraulic jacks (small force on small piston creates large force on large piston), and hydraulic machinery.

questions.text: An aeroplane's wing generates lift based on:
options: Newton's Third Law only
Bernoulli's principle — faster air above wing creates lower pressure
Archimedes' principle — plane displaces air
Pascal's Law — pressure applied to air is transmitted upward
questions.explanation: An aerofoil (wing) is curved on top and flatter below. Air moves faster over the curved top surface (greater path length), creating lower pressure above. Slower air below creates higher pressure. The pressure difference produces net upward lift. This is Bernoulli's theorem: P + ½ρv² = constant → higher v means lower P.

questions.text: The viscosity of a liquid generally:
options: Increases with temperature
Decreases with temperature
Is unaffected by temperature
First increases then decreases with temperature
questions.explanation: Viscosity of liquids DECREASES with temperature — thermal energy weakens intermolecular bonds that resist flow (honey flows more easily when warm). This is opposite for gases: gas viscosity INCREASES with temperature (more molecular collisions increase momentum transfer between layers). This difference is a classic exam trap.

questions.text: When a body is taken from the surface to the bottom of a lake, its weight:
options: Increases because of water pressure
Decreases because buoyancy acts upward, reducing apparent weight
Remains the same as weight is a constant
Becomes zero at sufficient depth
questions.explanation: True weight (mg) is constant regardless of location in water. But APPARENT weight (what a scale reads) = True weight − Buoyant force. Since buoyant force acts upward, apparent weight < true weight when submerged. An object weighs less on a spring balance when submerged in water than in air. This is why gold appears lighter when submerged (Archimedes' crown problem).

questions.text: Why does hot water clean better than cold water (aside from chemical effects)?
options: Hot water has higher density, exerting more force on dirt
Hot water has lower surface tension, allowing it to penetrate fabric fibres more easily
Heat melts dirt particles directly
Hot water has higher viscosity, trapping more dirt
questions.explanation: Surface tension decreases with temperature. Cold water's high surface tension makes it 'bead up' on surfaces. Hot water has lower surface tension and spreads more easily, penetrating fabric fibres and reaching dirt more effectively. Detergents further reduce surface tension (surfactants) — the combination of heat and detergent is most effective.

questions.text: Mercury shows a convex meniscus in a glass tube while water shows a concave meniscus because:
options: Mercury is denser than water
For mercury, cohesion (Hg-Hg) > adhesion (Hg-glass); for water, adhesion (water-glass) > cohesion (water-water)
Mercury is a metal and doesn't obey normal surface tension rules
The glass tube diameter is different for each
questions.explanation: Meniscus shape depends on relative strength of adhesion (liquid-glass) vs cohesion (liquid-liquid). Water: adhesion to glass > cohesion between water molecules → water wets glass, concave meniscus, rises in capillary. Mercury: cohesion between Hg atoms > adhesion to glass → mercury doesn't wet glass, convex meniscus, is depressed in capillary.

questions.text: A steel needle can float on water if placed carefully. This is due to:
options: Steel density equals water density
Surface tension of water supports the needle
The needle is hollow inside
Magnetic repulsion from Earth
questions.explanation: A steel needle (density ~7,800 kg/m³, far denser than water) cannot float by buoyancy. But if placed gently on the water surface, it deforms the surface tension 'skin' without breaking through. The surface tension provides an upward force sufficient to support the needle's weight. Once the surface is punctured, the needle immediately sinks.

questions.text: A submarine changes its depth by:
options: Changing its propeller speed only
Filling or emptying ballast tanks with seawater to change buoyancy
Changing the shape of its hull to alter displacement
Using a pump to push water downward
questions.explanation: Submarines use ballast tanks. To dive: seawater floods ballast tanks → submarine weight increases beyond buoyant force → submarine sinks. To surface: compressed air forces water out of tanks → weight decreases below buoyant force → submarine rises. At depth: weight = buoyancy (neutral buoyancy). This is a direct application of Archimedes' Principle.

questions.text: Water boils at a lower temperature at high altitudes because:
options: Less oxygen is available at high altitude
Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, lowering the boiling point
The air is colder at altitude, reducing heat input
High altitude water has fewer dissolved minerals
questions.explanation: Boiling point is the temperature at which vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At higher altitudes, atmospheric pressure is lower. Therefore, water needs to reach a lower vapour pressure to boil — which occurs at a lower temperature. At Everest base camp (~5,400 m), water boils at ~83°C instead of 100°C. Food takes longer to cook, and pressure cookers are used to raise the boiling point.

questions.text: Terminal velocity of a falling body through a viscous fluid is the velocity at which:
options: The body reaches maximum speed before hitting the ground
The net downward force (weight − buoyancy − drag) becomes zero
The body changes direction
Drag force equals weight only
questions.explanation: Terminal velocity: weight (downward) = buoyancy (upward) + drag force (upward, Stokes' Law: 6πηrv). When these balance, net force = 0 → no more acceleration → constant velocity = terminal velocity. v_t = 2r²(ρ_body − ρ_fluid)g/(9η). Larger spheres and denser bodies have higher terminal velocities; more viscous fluids give lower terminal velocities.

questions.text: Capillary action in plant stems (rise of water from roots to leaves) works because:
options: Plants actively pump water using electrical signals
Adhesion between water and xylem walls and cohesion between water molecules pull water upward
Osmotic pressure alone drives water up
The leaves create a partial vacuum drawing water up
questions.explanation: Water rises in narrow xylem vessels through a combination of: (1) Capillary action — adhesion of water to xylem walls + cohesion between water molecules (COHESION-TENSION theory). (2) Root pressure (osmosis in roots pushes water up). (3) Transpiration pull from leaves creates tension that draws water up the stem. Capillary action alone cannot account for trees over ~10 m, so transpiration pull is the main mechanism.

questions.text: A body floats in a liquid when:
options: Its density equals the liquid density
Its weight equals the buoyant force (weight of liquid displaced)
Its volume is greater than the liquid volume
The liquid density is less than the body density
questions.explanation: Floating condition: Weight of body = Weight of liquid displaced (= Buoyant force). This means the body displaces liquid equal to its own weight. If the body is less dense than the liquid, it displaces only part of its volume to equal its own weight — it floats partially submerged. If same density, it floats fully submerged (neutral buoyancy).

questions.text: The instrument used to measure the specific gravity (relative density) of liquids is:
options: Barometer
Manometer
Hydrometer
Thermometer
questions.explanation: A hydrometer measures the specific gravity (ratio of density to that of water) of liquids based on how deep it sinks (buoyancy principle). It is used to test milk purity (lactometer is a specialised hydrometer), battery acid concentration, and alcohol content. A barometer measures atmospheric pressure.

questions.text: In Bernoulli's equation (P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant), when fluid velocity increases, pressure:
options: Increases proportionally
Decreases
Remains unchanged
Becomes zero
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant. If v increases (fluid speeds up), ½ρv² increases, so P must decrease (assuming constant height h). This counter-intuitive result — faster flow means lower pressure — is the basis of lift in aeroplane wings, the curveball in cricket/baseball, and carburetors.

questions.text: The standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is approximately:
options: 760 mmHg or 101,325 Pa
500 mmHg or 66,661 Pa
1000 mmHg or 133,322 Pa
760 Pa
questions.explanation: Standard atmospheric pressure = 101,325 Pa = 1 atm = 760 mmHg (Torr) = 1.013 bar = 14.7 psi. This is the pressure exerted by a 760 mm column of mercury (as demonstrated by Torricelli's barometer in 1643). At high altitudes, atmospheric pressure decreases; in deep mines, it increases.
